> Body of commit message is a few lines of text, explaining things in more
> detail, possibly giving some background about the issue being fixed, etc.
>
> The body of the commit message can be several paragraphs, and
> please do proper word-wrap and keep columns shorter than about
> 74 characters or so. That way "git log" will show things
> nicely even when it's indented.
>
> Make sure you explain your solution and why you're doing what you're
> doing, as opposed to describing what you're doing. Reviewers and your
> future self can read the patch, but might not understand why a
> particular solution was implemented.
>
> Link to external resources[1], but again be sure enough relevant
> context from the external content is in the message to understand
> why it is relevant.
>
> [1] https://external.resources/
>
> Resolves: {LINK TO TICKET}
> OR
> Relates-to: {LINK TO TICKET}

## Process checklists

### Pre-review request

- [ ] I have provided a clear title and description of the changes, with
      motivation and rationale for why things need change, notes on obvious
      alternatives considered, and links to related work. Formatted according to
      project guidelines.
- [ ] I have personally reviewed every line change.
- [ ] I have provided evidence the changes function correctly.
- [ ] I possess the copyright to the changes such that they can be licensed
      under the project's terms.

### Pre-merge

- [ ] I have ensured the title and description reflect the final state of
      changes.
- [ ] I have tested and confirmed final changes function correctly.

## Testing

> Screenshots, GIF demos, code examples or output to help show the changes
> working as expected. ProTip: you can drag and drop or paste images into this
> textbox.
